plainMeaning: This line simply recites the names of ten more great, virtuous women — Rājimatī, Ṛṣidattā, Padmāvatī, Añjanā, Śrīdevī, Jyeṣṭhā, Sujyeṣṭhā, Mṛgāvatī, Prabhāvatī and Cellaṇā — so we remember and honour them.
recitation: rāimaï risidattā, paümāvaï aṁjaṇā sirīdevī | jiṭṭha sujiṭṭha migāvaï, pabhāvaï cillaṇādevī ||

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit rāimaï < Sanskrit Rājimatī ('she of the row/streak of light'); Rāī- < rāji + -matī.
wordByWord.notes: The bride of Tīrthaṅkara Neminātha who renounced when he renounced; counted among the soḷ satī (sixteen Satīs).

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit risidattā < Sanskrit Ṛṣidattā ('given by / gift of a sage'); ṛṣi + dattā.
wordByWord.notes: A kathā-tradition satī famed for enduring false accusation with steadfast śīla; story details vary by recension.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit paümāvaï < Sanskrit Padmāvatī ('lotus-lady'); paüma (padma) + -vatī, with characteristic Prakrit dm > üm softening.
wordByWord.notes: Several Padmāvatīs appear in the kathā corpus; the precise figure intended here is not fixed across recensions — see notes.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit aṁjaṇā < Sanskrit Añjanā ('the dark/collyrium-hued one'); from añj- 'to anoint'.
wordByWord.notes: Wife of Pavanañjaya and mother of Hanumān in the Jain Rāmāyaṇa; archetype of unwavering virtue through long unjust abandonment.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit sirī < Sanskrit śrī ('splendour, fortune') + devī ('lady, queen'); -devī here is an honorific suffix.
wordByWord.notes: A less prominently narrated satī; identification varies and is flagged as uncertain in notes.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit jiṭṭha < Sanskrit Jyeṣṭhā ('eldest'); Skt. conjunct -yeṣṭh- regularly becomes Prakrit -iṭṭh-.
wordByWord.notes: Reckoned a daughter of King Cetaka of Vaiśālī and sister of Cellaṇā.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit sujiṭṭha < Sanskrit Sujyeṣṭhā ('the truly/well eldest'); prefix su- ('good, well') + jyeṣṭhā.
wordByWord.notes: Also counted a daughter of Cetaka; some recensions treat her as a separate sister, others as a variant of Jyeṣṭhā — flagged.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit migāvaï < Sanskrit Mṛgāvatī ('she of the doe', doe-eyed); miga (mṛga) + -vatī.
wordByWord.notes: Queen of Kauśāmbī, wife of King Śatānīka; later a sādhvī under Mahāvīra who attained kevala-jñāna; one of the soḷ satī.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit pabhāvaï < Sanskrit Prabhāvatī ('the radiant one'); pabhā (prabhā, 'lustre') + -vatī, Skt. pr- > Prakrit p-.
wordByWord.notes: Queen of King Udāyana of Vītabhaya, a renowned devotee linked to the sandalwood image of Mahāvīra.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit cillaṇā (Cellaṇā) + devī ('queen', honorific suffix); name of non-Sanskritic/regional origin.
wordByWord.notes: Daughter of Cetaka and chief queen of King Śreṇika (Bimbisāra) of Magadha; a devout follower of Mahāvīra; one of the soḷ satī. The verse-final -devī mirrors sirīdevī, closing the line on an honorific.

literalTranslation: Rājimatī, Ṛṣidattā, Padmāvatī, Añjanā, Śrīdevī; Jyeṣṭhā, Sujyeṣṭhā, Mṛgāvatī, Prabhāvatī, and Queen Cellaṇā.
idiomaticTranslation: Rājimatī and Ṛṣidattā; Padmāvatī, Añjanā, and Lady Śrīdevī; Jyeṣṭhā, Sujyeṣṭhā, and Mṛgāvatī; Prabhāvatī, and Queen Cellaṇā.

elaboration.verseByVerseCommentary: The roll-call of satīs continues, and this gāthā quietly widens its social and narrative range. Where the previous verse drew on figures like Sulasā and Candanabālā, here the named women span every station of lay life: a renouncing bride (Rājimatī, who turned from marriage to Neminātha straight to the ascetic path), a wronged and vindicated wife (Ṛṣidattā), a forest-abandoned mother (Añjanā of the Jain Rāmāyaṇa), and a cluster of royal queens — Mṛgāvatī of Kauśāmbī, Prabhāvatī of Vītabhaya, and Cellaṇā of Magadha. The pairing of Jyeṣṭhā and Sujyeṣṭhā, with Cellaṇā, keeps the daughters of Cetaka of Vaiśālī together, so a single illustrious household is honoured within the litany. The line's craft is its plainness: ten names, no verbs, no adjectives — the reciter is meant to supply each remembered life from the heart. Notice the deliberate symmetry of the two -devī endings, sirīdevī closing the first half-line and cillaṇādevī sealing the verse, framing the catalogue with the dignity of 'queen/lady.' What unites these very different women is not rank or circumstance but unbroken śīla held through betrayal, exile, abduction, and renunciation alike — the one thread the whole sajjhāy is binding into the reciter's resolve.
elaboration.doctrinalContext: The verse embodies the Jain conviction that śīla and samyak-darśana, not gender or worldly role, determine spiritual worth: a discarded wife and a reigning queen stand on one line because both kept conduct intact. Several of these names (Rājimatī, Mṛgāvatī, Cellaṇā) belong to the fixed Śvetāmbara group of soḷ satī, sixteen Satīs held as unchanging exemplars — making this gāthā a point of contact between the sajjhāy's open litany and that canonical sixteen. Mṛgāvatī's path from queen to kevalin further illustrates the doctrine that the householder's life is no bar to the highest attainment.
elaboration.practicalRelevance: Recited before dawn, this line invites the practitioner to pause on each name and recall not a deed to admire but a virtue to imitate — patience under false blame (Ṛṣidattā), steadiness in abandonment (Añjanā), guarding of one's integrity against power (Mṛgāvatī), devotion amid royal comfort (Prabhāvatī, Cellaṇā) — and to carry that one quality into the day's conduct.

notes: The verse is a pure name-list with no particles or verbs, so commentary is calibrated tight. Token glosses are secure as proper nouns; the precise narrative identities behind the less-storied names (Padmāvatī, Śrīdevī) vary across recensions and are flagged as uncertain rather than invented. Jyeṣṭhā/Sujyeṣṭhā are treated by some traditions as two sisters and by others as variants of one figure. Per the tradition knowledge base, the source book's verbatim orthography (e.g. the conjunct in જિટ્ઠ/સુજિટ્ઠ, the -ઇ vocalism of રાઇમઇ/પઉમાવઇ/મિગાવઇ/પભાવઇ) is preserved and not normalized. The daṇḍa (।) and verse-number bars are book punctuation, not lexical tokens. Web auto-summaries that equate these satīs with Tīrthaṅkara mothers were rejected as unreliable, consistent with the preface's caution.
